original_comment: Major revision invited; provide a line-by-line response and outline every change.
action_required: Create atomic tracker coverage and synchronize manuscript, appendix, rebuttal, change log, unresolved log, and build record.
action_taken: Expanded the tracker to atomic IDs, rewrote the manuscript around the seed summaries, tightened the appendix audit and transfer text, bundled the Elsevier support files into paper/tex, completed a successful local latexmk build, and synchronized the rebuttal draft and logs.
rebuttal_draft: We converted the review into an atomic response matrix, tied each substantive manuscript change to specific evidence files, and verified a successful local build of the submission package.
unresolved_reason: A small set of evidence-limit items remains partial: R1-HR5, R1-WX1, R1-WX4, and R1-Q4.

original_comment: Verify that the revised manuscript compiles locally and document the final build path for submission.
action_required: Vendor the Elsevier support files into the paper project, compile locally, inspect the log, and update the submission checklist.
action_taken: Vendored cas-sc.cls, cas-common.sty, and cas-model2-names.bst into paper/tex, ran latexmk -pdf -bibtex main.tex successfully under texlive/20220321, and updated the checklist to distinguish successful local compilation from the remaining minor layout warnings.
rebuttal_draft: We verified a successful local build of the standalone paper package and documented the exact command sequence and remaining minor layout warnings in the updated checklist.

original_comment: The paper makes progressive allocation appear emergent even though the reviewer argues this may be imposed by allocation bounds and should be compared against non-progressive cases.
manuscript_locations: Abstract; Introduction; Policy Analysis Results; Cross-Policy Synthesis; Conclusions
action_required: State the true bounds and remove any wording that treats progressive allocation as hard-coded or emergent from unequal constraints.
action_taken: Rewrote the manuscript to state that all 12 allocation variables currently use 0.0-0.8 bounds and that low-income-favoring outcomes are empirical results of the revised experiments.
rebuttal_draft: The revised paper now reports the low-income-favoring pattern as an observed outcome under uniform bounds rather than as a consequence of imposed progressive constraints.

original_comment: The reviewer asks whether the study is mono-objective or multi-objective and notes that the optimization framing is unclear.
action_required: Clarify that the workflow uses separate single-objective runs followed by cross-policy synthesis.
action_taken: Rewrote the abstract, methods, and conclusion to describe three separate single-objective Bayesian optimisations plus a comparative synthesis stage.
rebuttal_draft: We now describe the workflow explicitly as three single-objective optimisation studies followed by a comparative synthesis, not as a joint multi-objective optimiser.

original_comment: The reviewer asks how stochasticity was handled and whether simulations were repeated enough to support robust conclusions.
manuscript_locations: Abstract; Optimisation Methodology; Policy Analysis Results; Conclusions
action_required: Replace single-run claims with repeated-seed summaries and explain deterministic control.
action_taken: Rewrote the methods and results around the 30-seed merged summaries and added the deterministic fixed-policy control as a mechanism check for stochastic irregularity.
rebuttal_draft: The revised manuscript reports means, 95 percent confidence intervals, and deterministic-control interpretation instead of relying on single-run objective traces.

original_comment: The reviewer asks why the metrics are not unified in scale and requests explicit metric units and interpretation.
manuscript_locations: Framework metric definitions; Optimisation Methodology; Cross-Policy Synthesis
action_required: State units and interpretation for each metric and avoid implying raw-scale comparability.
action_taken: Revised the metric definitions so mode-share equity is described as dimensionless, travel-time equity as minutes, and total system travel time as minutes; clarified that cross-objective rankings use explicit normalization only for decision support.
rebuttal_draft: We now report the units and interpretive scope of each metric explicitly and confine cross-metric comparisons to labelled normalized views.

original_comment: The reviewer asks whether the model has been presented previously and whether an open-source implementation can be provided for reproducibility.
action_required: Add a code-availability statement and describe the reproducibility workflow accurately.
action_taken: Added a code-availability and reproducibility paragraph with the GitHub repository URL and the revised evidence workflow.
rebuttal_draft: We added the repository URL and described the scripts, merged summaries, and deterministic-control workflow used in the revision.
unresolved_reason: Public visibility and archival release details were not independently verified from the local environment.

original_comment: The ABM design and implementation must be clearly described and validated before drawing conclusions.
action_required: Clarify model scope, agent interactions, and the current status of validation.
action_taken: Rewrote the introduction and framework sections to describe the model as a stylised subsidy-allocation scenario tool, regenerated Figure 1 to remove a misleading visual cue, and replaced the old validation table with a clearer calibration and internal-checks discussion.
rebuttal_draft: The revised manuscript now distinguishes implementation audit, repeated-seed robustness, deterministic control, and a corrected architecture schematic from full empirical calibration.
unresolved_reason: A full external empirical validation study is still outside the current evidence package.

original_comment: The model only considers shared cars, shared bikes, and public transport; why are private cars and private bikes not considered?
action_required: Explain the modal scope and why private ownership modes are excluded from the current policy instrument.
action_taken: Added explicit text that the current study concerns subsidies delivered through MaaS and service-based modes, not ownership, parking, or fuel policies.
rebuttal_draft: We clarified that private vehicle ownership is outside the current instrument design and listed it as an important extension for future work.

original_comment: The simulation setup is not sufficiently clear: why Niter = 144, what are the 10 initial matrices, how was the fictional environment defined, and how many repetitions were done?
action_required: Separate simulation time steps from BO iterations and document the current environment and seed workflow.
action_taken: Rewrote the methods and appendix to state the 100x100 grid, 200 commuters, 144 within-run time steps, 8 Latin-hypercube initial samples, 10 or 3 BO updates by objective, and 30-seed evaluation.
rebuttal_draft: The revised paper now explains the sampled FPS set, BO initialization, objective-specific BO updates, simulation horizon, and repeated-seed evaluation clearly.

original_comment: References and sources are missing for many parameter values and assumptions, including value of time, congestion parameters, sensitivity coefficients, network parameters, benefits assumptions, and Australian data used for monetised benefits.
appendix_locations: Parameter Specifications; Calibration and Validation; Competing Benefits Calculation
action_required: Audit which values are script-defined, which are externally sourced, and remove unsupported benefit assumptions.
action_taken: Replaced the appendix parameter sections with audited script values, documented the scripted commuter-attribute weights and preserved PBS resource requests, removed the unsupported monetized-benefit claims from the manuscript, and converted the appendix competing-benefits section into a status note.
rebuttal_draft: We now separate audited implementation parameters, preserved job-resource records, and external calibration claims, and we no longer rely on unsupported monetized-benefit numbers.
unresolved_reason: The revision still does not add external literature support for every behavioural parameter because the current evidence package is primarily an implementation audit.

original_comment: The description of Algorithm 1 is incomplete; define S, P, A, and E and explain the time scale of pricing and BPR routing updates.
action_required: Clarify the vector definitions and distinguish exogenous environment from endogenous demand.
action_taken: Revised the main text and appendix to define S, P, A, and E more clearly, explain that demand is endogenous, and note that pricing and routing updates occur within the 144-step simulation horizon.
rebuttal_draft: The revised appendix now distinguishes exogenous environment variables from endogenous trip generation and clarifies within-run update timing.

original_comment: Table 6 baseline modal share distributions by income group appear unrealistic and do not sum to 100%; explain why.
action_required: Remove or explicitly quarantine unsupported baseline mode-share claims.
action_taken: Removed the legacy baseline mode-share table and rewrote the results around the seed-based summaries instead of the incomplete export.
rebuttal_draft: We removed the incomplete baseline table from the manuscript rather than defending a partial export as a full mode split.

original_comment: Explain why optimal subsidy allocation pushes low-income groups toward shared bikes, shared cars, and public transport while pushing high-income groups away from cars, and report how trip counts vary with subsidies.
action_required: Interpret the updated allocation patterns and use trip-summary evidence to explain them.
action_taken: Rewrote the results to report the dominant allocation components at the revised optima and added trip-count and average-trip-time evidence for the total-system analysis.
rebuttal_draft: We now discuss which allocation cells dominate each objective-specific optimum and use the trip summary to show that efficiency changes come from moderate time reductions rather than dramatic trip growth.

original_comment: Do the reported subsidy-use shares by income group simply reflect group sizes, and why are some subsidies not used?
manuscript_locations: Mode Share Equity Results; Policy Implications
action_required: Clarify the difference between population share, allocation share, and realised subsidy use, and explain budget saturation.
action_taken: Added text stating that aggregate subsidy shares are not mechanically identical to population shares and that declining budget use reflects saturation rather than accounting error.
rebuttal_draft: The revised results now distinguish allocation shares from group sizes and explain unused subsidies as a saturation effect in a bounded-demand system.

original_comment: Is the recommended range consistent with previous findings and can equity improve by worsening outcomes for others?
action_required: Replace the old 54-72 range with the revised seed-based ranges and discuss the interpretation more cautiously.
action_taken: Replaced the old range with seed-based ranges, added explicit wording that a better equity score is not treated as proof of a Pareto improvement, and linked the scenario-specific interpretation to transport-equity scholarship that prioritises disadvantaged groups rather than equal nominal benefits.
rebuttal_draft: We now present the revised FPS recommendations as ranges or plateaus, make the cross-group trade-offs explicit, and tie the interpretation back to the transport-equity literature already cited in the manuscript.

original_comment: The title is too broad because the article evaluates subsidy allocation policies only.
action_required: Narrow the title to subsidy-allocation scope.
action_taken: Changed the title to ABM-ETOP: An Agent-Based Framework for Evaluating Multimodal Subsidy Allocation Policies for Equity and Efficiency.
rebuttal_draft: The title now reflects the actual policy scope of the paper.

original_comment: The paper claims ABM-ETOP overcomes limitations of other models as validated through comparisons, but no such comparisons are presented.
action_required: Tone down the comparison claim and describe Table 2 as conceptual rather than empirical validation.
action_taken: Rewrote the ABM-ETOP positioning paragraph to describe the table as a conceptual capability comparison rather than a validated benchmark.
rebuttal_draft: We removed the unsupported claim of validation through direct comparisons.

original_comment: The paper appears to equate horizontal equity with total system travel time or system-wide efficiency without sufficient justification.
action_required: Separate equity-focused metrics from the efficiency metric and stop treating TSTT as a direct horizontal-equity index.
action_taken: Revised the metric definitions and results language to describe TSTT as a system-efficiency outcome rather than as a full horizontal-equity measure.
rebuttal_draft: The revised manuscript now distinguishes clearly between equity-focused outcomes and the efficiency outcome.

original_comment: The paper claims to address the implementation gap in transport policy, but the scenario is too simplified to support that claim.
manuscript_locations: Introduction; Policy Implications; Methodological Contribution; Limitations
action_required: Remove or qualify implementation-gap claims and reposition the framework as stylised scenario analysis.
action_taken: Rewrote the introduction, implications, and methodological-contribution sections to position ABM-ETOP as a stylised subsidy-allocation analysis framework rather than a closed implementation-gap solution.
rebuttal_draft: We now present the framework as a stylised scenario-analysis tool and treat empirical implementation as future work.

original_comment: How does the framework transfer to city scale?
action_required: State the current transfer limits and what would be required for city-scale use.
action_taken: Added explicit transfer text stating that city-scale use would require an empirical network, local fare and demand data, calibrated behavioural parameters, and rerunning the seed-based and deterministic checks on that case.
rebuttal_draft: We now answer the city-scale question directly by outlining the required empirical-network, demand, calibration, and robustness steps without claiming that the present stylised case is already city-scale.

original_comment: Is the approach applicable to real-world cases and how?
action_required: Explain how the workflow could be ported to empirical cases without overstating readiness.
action_taken: Added an illustrative implementation workflow based on local data assembly, empirical calibration, repeated-seed stress testing, ex-post monitoring, and rerunning the deterministic control as an interpretive check on the empirical case.
rebuttal_draft: The revised paper now explains how the workflow can be ported to an empirical case, while still stating clearly that the current manuscript reports a stylised scenario rather than an executed real-world deployment.

original_comment: Is it a good recommendation to encourage car services in cities with severe congestion?
action_required: Add a caution that observed car-service support in the stylised scenario does not imply a universal recommendation for congested cities.
action_taken: Added explicit cautionary text that car-service support in the current scenario should not be generalized to congested cities without local network and emissions testing.
rebuttal_draft: We now state explicitly that the car-service pattern is scenario-specific and must be re-tested under local congestion and emissions constraints.

original_comment: Can the paper give precise numbers for how many policies were explored, in what time, and with what computational power?
action_required: Document the number of FPS values, dimensions, initial samples, BO updates, and any recoverable compute-environment details; report runtime only if recoverable.
action_taken: Added exact evaluation counts to the appendix, documented the preserved PBS job-resource requests, and added recoverable measured walltimes from preserved scheduler outputs for the deterministic jobs and representative total-system seed-array jobs.
rebuttal_draft: We now report exact policy-count and evaluation-count figures, preserved resource requests, and the measured walltimes that can be recovered safely from local scheduler logs.
unresolved_reason: Measured walltimes are recoverable only for the preserved deterministic jobs and representative total-system seed-array jobs, not for every optimisation objective in the revised package.

original_comment: Are commuter-agent attributes internally consistent, for example age and disability?
action_required: State the implemented commuter attributes and clarify how the behavioural rules use them.
action_taken: Expanded the commuter-agent description to include the income attribute, the audited marginal weights for health/payment/disability/tech access, and the implemented penalty hooks for disability, age/health, and no-tech-access cases.
rebuttal_draft: We now explain that the current model samples commuter attributes from weighted marginals and applies them through explicit penalty and pricing rules, while identifying the population as stylised rather than jointly calibrated.

action_taken: Regenerated Figure 1 as a manuscript-local schematic with consistent allocation encoding and updated the caption to explain that darker cells and larger circles indicate higher illustrative allocation percentages.

rebuttal_draft: The revised caption now explains why utilisation spikes need not imply proportional instantaneous price spikes.
